Party_Details.Name: Leong Hin Chuee

Facts.Text: Before entering into the Employment Agreement with the Transpacific Group, Leong Hin Chuee held the position of Executive Vice-President and Head (Business Development and Investor Relations) at Lereno Bio-Chem Ltd, a Catalist-listed company in Singapore.
On 20 November 2007, at Lereno Bio-Chem Ltd, Leong Hin Chuee met Suganda Setiadikurnia, who was then Vice-Chairman of Lereno, after Suganda subscribed for a significant number of Lereno shares.

On 1 August 2007, Leong Hin Chuee entered into an Employment Agreement (the EA) signed by D7 on behalf of the Group, which was described in the EA as 'a Group of companies incorporated in Indonesia and Singapore with its principal place of business at the 18th floor of Menara Imperium, Jakarta Indonesia'.
Leong Hin Chuee received a monthly salary of S$25,000 pursuant to the Employment Agreement dated 1 August 2007 from 1 August 2007 to July 2010.

The Employment Agreement dated 1 August 2007 provided for Annual Bonuses, Periodic Bonuses, share rewards and reimbursement of expenses under clauses 5.3, 5.4, 5.5 and 5.9, subject to specified conditions.

Clause 5.3 stated that the Executive shall be paid an Annual Bonus and may be paid Periodic Bonuses such as the 0.25% to 0.5% relation to various Fund Raising Activities as verbally expressed by the CEO prior to the signing of this Agreement, in cash or shares or a combination of both, the quantum of which shall be at the sole and absolute discretion of the CEO and which shall depend on the performance of the Company and the attainment of the objectives as determined by the CEO.

Clause 5.4 stated that upon completion of the Project Gibson RTO/IPO, Executive shall be awarded full title and legal ownership of a minimum of 12 million shares in the listed company, and shall pay only/earn-out for the Project Gibson shares according to Table 1 if the Executive ceases to be employed by the Company for any reason whatsoever.

Clause 5.5 stated that upon completion of the Project MP RTO/IPO, Executive shall be awarded full title and legal ownership of a minimum of 12 million shares in the listed company, and shall pay only/earn-out for the Project MP shares according to Table 1 if the Executive ceases to be employed by the Company for any reason whatsoever.

Clause 5.9 stated that the Executive shall be provided with Company Transportation and reasonable Accommodations while in Jakarta or other locations as assigned by the CEO, and shall also be reimbursed for reasonable traveling, hotel accommodations, telecommunications and other out-of-pocket expenses incurred in or about the discharge of the Executive's duties.
Clauses 5.4 and 5.5 were amended by having '1.5% for 100%' scribbled next to them and D7 countersigning at the side.

Leong Hin Chuee understood the handwritten notation "1.5% for 100%" next to clauses 5.4 and 5.5 and the accompanying Table 1 to mean that he would receive 1.5% of the shares of RODA and CKRA or their cash equivalent when Project Gibson and Project MP were completed.

The Employment Agreement described the counterparty as the Transpacific Group, a group of companies incorporated in Indonesia and Singapore with its principal place of business at the 18th floor of Menara Imperium, Jakarta Indonesia, and was signed by D7 on behalf of the Group.

Clause 2.1.1 stated that the plaintiff shall undertake such duties and exercise such powers in relation to the Company and its business as directed by Suganda Setiadikurnia or his successors or assigns, and that the responsibilities and duties must be legal and reasonably consistent with the Executive's designation.

Clause 2.1.2 stated that the plaintiff's employment should accord with all resolutions, regulations, policies and directions given by D7, and clause 2.1.3 stated that D7 would have sole direction of the location of the plaintiff's employment.
The Employment Agreement contemplated that the plaintiff could be assigned to holding companies, subsidiary companies, associates and other parties to which D7 may assign or vest duties and powers.

The Employment Agreement referred to Project Gibson Stock Earn-out and Ownership upon completion of the Project Gibson RTO/IPO and Project MP Stock Earn-out and Ownership upon completion of the Project MP RTO/IPO.
On 31 August 2007, Leong Hin Chuee resigned from his position at Lereno Bio-Chem Ltd.

After the Employment Agreement, Leong Hin Chuee was appointed as a director of Cozmo International Pte Ltd, Cozmo Properties Pte Ltd, Fook Yuan International Pte Ltd and Fortune International Trading Pte Ltd, companies controlled by Suganda Setiadikurnia.
Pursuant to the Employment Agreement, Leong Hin Chuee was appointed Deputy President Director of PT Royal Oak Development Asia.
Pursuant to the Employment Agreement, Leong Hin Chuee was appointed President Director of PT Citra Kebun Raya Agri.
In January 2008, a first rights issue of CKRA shares was conducted as part of Project MP, with proceeds used to purchase mandatory exchangeable bonds issued by PT Kurnia Selaras.

Under the Project MP structure, CKRA was to conduct a rights issue, use the proceeds to purchase mandatory exchangeable bonds from PT Kurnia Selaras, receive 1% interest annually for five years until 28 January 2013, and upon maturity exchange the bonds for 70% equity in PT Transpacific Agro Industry and PT Citra Indoniaga.

Under the Project MP plan, after the first CKRA rights issue and acquisitions, a second rights issue was to be conducted, with 78% of proceeds to fund the purchase of PT Horizon Agro Industry and 22% for CKRA working capital.

Project MP planning documents contemplated three scenarios for the second CKRA rights issue: a best-case scenario of up to 300 billion rupiah raised from investors, an expected scenario of up to 200 billion rupiah, and a worst-case scenario of no investor funds, requiring a pledge of 400 billion rupiah worth of shares to secure 100 billion rupiah of loans.
As part of Project MP, Citra Group Pte Ltd was to be incorporated as an investment holding company to act as standby buyer underwriting the second CKRA rights issue.

To fulfil underwriting obligations for the second CKRA rights issue under Project MP, Citra Group Pte Ltd later entered into a loan facility with the Singapore branch of Raiffeisen Zentralbank Oesterreich AG for US$111,663,482.44, referred to as the First RZB Loan.
In planning for Project MP, Leong Hin Chuee envisaged that CKRA could become a multi-billion dollar company within three years based on the rights issues and acquisitions.

In relation to Project MP, Leong Hin Chuee engaged with Raiffeisen Zentralbank Oesterreich AG regarding financing, with the objective of having the bank accept the collateral and proposed financing structure for the First RZB Loan.
By 31 December 2010, Citra Group Pte Ltd held approximately 78.76% of CKRA shares, with CKRA shares being freely tradable on the Indonesian Stock Exchange.
Under Project Gibson, Forever Prosperous Pte Ltd was to be incorporated as an investment holding company to subscribe for and hold shares in PT Royal Oak Development Asia.

Project Gibson envisaged that RODA would conduct a rights issue around January 2008, with 40% of proceeds to acquire apartment and retail units in Oakwood Premier Cozmo Jakarta from PT Adhisakti Kreasi Persada and 50% of proceeds to buy shares in PT Transpacific Mutual Capita.

Because RODA's rights issue subscription had to be in cash under Indonesian Stock Exchange regulations and the purchasing parties lacked sufficient cash, Forever Prosperous Pte Ltd borrowed US$115,999,561.97 from DBS Bank under a one-day loan facility to subscribe for RODA shares.

Under the Project Gibson plan, the parties envisaged that RODA would be relisted, assets injected into RODA would be monetised, and Forever Prosperous Pte Ltd would benefit from an increase in the value of its RODA shareholding, with a view to future sale of those shares to third parties.

In connection with Project Gibson, Leong Hin Chuee engaged in discussions with DBS Bank regarding a loan facility for Forever Prosperous Pte Ltd, presenting a financing structure and collateral arrangement to support the bank's decision.

Following the DBS loan facility, Forever Prosperous Pte Ltd acquired approximately 50.2% of RODA shares, with the balance of freely tradable RODA shares held by other entities within the Transpacific Group.

After the Employment Agreement was signed, Suganda Setiadikurnia directed Leong Hin Chuee to raise funds for the acquisition of properties at 21 Anderson Road, referred to as the 21 Anderson Properties, as an additional assignment linked to Project Gibson.

For the 21 Anderson Properties acquisition, Fortune International Trading Pte Ltd was to secure a loan and then lend the funds to Cozmo Properties Pte Ltd and Springfield Ventures Group Ltd to purchase the properties.

Fortune International Trading Pte Ltd raised S$170.4 million via a loan from Raiffeisen Zentralbank Oesterreich AG, referred to as the Second RZB Loan, and on 15 April 2010 disbursed the loan proceeds to Cozmo Properties Pte Ltd and Springfield Ventures Group Ltd to complete the purchase of the 21 Anderson Properties.

In connection with the Second RZB Loan for the 21 Anderson Properties, Leong Hin Chuee provided a personal guarantee to Raiffeisen Zentralbank Oesterreich AG as part of the credit support for the facility.

Under the Second RZB Loan terms for the 21 Anderson Properties, Fortune International Trading Pte Ltd was maintain a maximum loan-to-value ratio approved by the RZB board, with non-compliance treated as an indication that the loan could not be repaid by 31 March 2011.

When the loan-to-value ratio condition under the Second RZB Loan was not maintained, Raiffeisen Zentralbank Oesterreich AG initiated steps to sell the 21 Anderson Properties to reduce the interest payable on the loan.

Following concerns about repayment of the Second RZB Loan, Leong Hin Chuee was assigned to seek additional funding sources and engaged with Arch Capital Management Co Ltd and Arch Capital Asian Partners LP regarding a potential purchase of the 21 Anderson Properties.

A transaction was structured in which Arch Capital Asian Partners LP would purchase the 21 Anderson Properties for S$200 million, with the proceeds to be applied towards repayment of the Second RZB Loan.

Cozmo Properties Pte Ltd and Springfield Ventures Group Ltd incurred an aggregate loss of approximately S$20 million in connection with the acquisition and subsequent sale of the 21 Anderson Properties at S$200 million.

Between 2008 and 2010, Leong Hin Chuee incurred expenses in the course of his work, including telephone bills, flights, Indonesian visa fees, equipment purchases, intern allowances, and entertainment and other expenses, totalling S$79,113.67.
Around mid-2010, Leong Hin Chuee submitted a consolidated reimbursement claim of S$79,113.67 for expenses incurred from 2008 to 2010 to Angel Setiadikurnia for processing.

In processing Leong Hin Chuee's reimbursement claim, Angel Setiadikurnia requested invoices and supporting documents, grouped them by year, assessed reasonableness, and forwarded them to PT Transpacific Securindo's Human Resources Department and the board for approval.

Following internal review, a sum of S$25,109.15 out of the S$79,113.67 reimbursement claim submitted by Leong Hin Chuee was approved and a cheque for S$25,109.15 was prepared and made available to him.
Leong Hin Chuee collected and banked in the cheque for S$25,109.15 that was issued in response to his reimbursement claim.

On 17 July 2010, in an email to Angel Setiadikurnia, Leong Hin Chuee stated that he was prepared to receive payment for work done until 30 June 2010 and that he must be repaid for expenses he had paid or incurred on Suganda Setiadikurnia's behalf, and that he was prepared to waive various fees and free shares described as due to him under his contract.

On 27 July 2010, in a further email to Angel Setiadikurnia, Leong Hin Chuee requested confirmation of a cross cheque for S$78,803.29 as final payment for all expenses and stated that he was prepared to waive 1.5% of 100% free shares on RODA and CKRA and 0.25% to 0.5% fees for net funds raised, as set out in his contract.
On 24 October 2010, Angel Setiadikurnia emailed Leong Hin Chuee stating that a cheque had been prepared about a month earlier for his expenses and that Cozmo had no outstanding amounts with him.
By late 2009, the December 2009 salary of S$25,000 for Leong Hin Chuee had not been paid on time, and his 2010 salary payments were made later than scheduled.

Around the time of his resignation in July 2010, Leong Hin Chuee proposed that if his expenses of S$79,113.67 were fully reimbursed, he would not pursue claims for bonuses, share rewards, and other entitlements which he calculated at more than S$10 million.

During his engagement with the Transpacific Group, Leong Hin Chuee worked primarily at the premises of PT Transpacific Securindo in Indonesia and held an employment pass issued under PT Transpacific Securindo to comply with Indonesian regulatory requirements.

Throughout the period of the Employment Agreement, Suganda Setiadikurnia exercised control over key aspects of Leong Hin Chuee’s work, including assignments, locations, project directions, and decisions on bonuses.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, on the terms of the Employment Agreement dated 1 August 2007 and the parties' pre-dispute conduct, Leong Hin Chuee is contractually entitled to payment of bonuses, share rewards, and expense reimbursements in respect of Project Gibson, Project MP, and the 21 Anderson Properties fund-raising activities, and whether any such entitlements were waived or satisfied.
Party_Details.Rule: Under contract law, the existence and scope of any entitlement to bonuses, share rewards, and expense reimbursements are determined by construing the written agreement objectively in its context, including express terms, amendments, and the parties' pre-dispute conduct. Discretionary bonus clauses are interpreted to identify whether they confer a contractual right to consideration of a bonus or to a specific quantum. Expense reimbursement clauses generally entitle an employee to repayment of reasonable expenses incurred in or about the discharge of contractual duties, subject to compliance with any agreed approval or documentation processes.
Party_Details.Application: The Employment Agreement expressly provided for a fixed monthly salary and contemplated variable performance bonuses, share rewards linked to the completion of specific corporate projects, and reimbursement of reasonable expenses. Before any dispute arose, the plaintiff was appointed to senior roles to execute large loan facilities enabling various corporate entities to underwrite rights issues and acquire shareholdings. The plaintiff incurred work-related expenses, submitted a consolidated reimbursement claim, and offered to waive his substantial bonus claims if the expenses were paid in full, though only partial reimbursement was ultimately approved. These pre-dispute events frame the contractual questions of whether the conditions for bonuses and share rewards—specifically the successful commercial completion of the projects—were satisfied, whether the unapproved portion of expenses met the contractual criteria for reasonableness, and whether his conditional waiver proposal took effect.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed the identity of the contracting parties and determined that the signatory, acting on behalf of a non-existent group, was personally bound by the Employment Agreement. On the merits of the expense claim, the court found that the unapproved expenses did not meet the contractual criteria of being reasonable and incurred in the discharge of duties. Regarding the bonuses and share rewards, the court interpreted the discretionary clauses and found that the contractual conditions, specifically the 'completion' of the relevant projects through successful divestment and monetisation, were not satisfied. The projects suffered from structural failures, including round-tripped funds and incomplete asset injections. Consequently, the contractual preconditions for the variable rewards were not met, and the claims for these entitlements were rejected.

Party_Details.Name: Suganda Setiadikurnia

Facts.Text: Suganda Setiadikurnia served as Vice-Chairman of Lereno Bio-Chem Ltd following his subscription for a significant number of shares in the company.
Suganda Setiadikurnia signed an Employment Agreement with the plaintiff on behalf of the 'Transpacific Group', an unincorporated collection of companies operating in Indonesia and Singapore.
Suganda Setiadikurnia directed the plaintiff to undertake various roles across multiple corporate entities he controlled to execute 'Project Gibson' and 'Project MP'.

Suganda Setiadikurnia oversaw the execution of Project MP and Project Gibson, which ultimately involved round-tripped funds, incomplete asset injections, and a failure to successfully divest shares to third-party investors.
Suganda Setiadikurnia did not exercise his contractual discretion to award the plaintiff the performance bonuses or share rewards referenced in the Employment Agreement.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether an individual who signs an employment agreement on behalf of an unincorporated group is personally liable for discretionary bonuses when the underlying corporate projects fail to achieve commercial completion.
Party_Details.Rule: A person who signs a contract on behalf of a non-existent entity or unincorporated group is personally bound by the contract. However, liability for discretionary bonuses tied to project completion requires the actual satisfaction of the defined completion metrics and a good-faith exercise of discretion.
Party_Details.Application: Suganda Setiadikurnia signed the Employment Agreement on behalf of the 'Transpacific Group', an entity that did not legally exist. He subsequently directed the executive's work across various specific corporate vehicles to execute complex financial projects. When the projects failed to monetize or achieve the planned divestments, he did not exercise discretion to award the bonuses or share rewards. The executive subsequently sought to enforce the bonus provisions against him personally.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that because the 'Transpacific Group' was not a legal entity, the individual signatory was personally bound by the employment contract. However, upon reviewing the merits of the contractual claims, the court interpreted the bonus and share reward clauses as strictly conditional upon the successful commercial completion and monetization of the projects. Since the projects involved round-tripped funds and failed to achieve the intended third-party divestments, the conditions precedent were not satisfied. Therefore, the refusal to pay the discretionary bonuses and share rewards was contractually justified, and the individual was not liable for the claims.

Party_Details.Name: Citra Group Pte Ltd and others

Facts.Text: The corporate defendants were companies incorporated in Singapore and utilized as investment holding vehicles for various acquisitions.
Following the execution of the Employment Agreement, the plaintiff was appointed as a director of several of these Singapore-incorporated entities to facilitate the execution of the projects.
None of the corporate defendants were signatories to the Employment Agreement, which was signed prior to the incorporation of some of the entities.
The corporate defendants were utilized to secure substantial loans from banks to underwrite rights issues and acquire shares as part of the overarching project structures.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether corporate entities can be held liable for an employment agreement they did not sign, and whether they can recover damages for a director's alleged breaches of duty during the execution of failed corporate projects.
Party_Details.Rule: A company is not bound by a contract unless it is a party to it or has validly ratified it under statutory provisions. While directors owe fiduciary duties to their companies, any corporate claim against a director requires concrete proof of an actual breach of duty and resulting financial loss.
Party_Details.Application: The corporate entities were utilized as operational vehicles for various acquisitions and rights issues. The executive served as a director for several of them, but his master employment contract was signed prior to their incorporation by a different party. Following the failure of the projects to monetize, the executive attempted to claim bonuses directly from these entities. Simultaneously, the entities pursued claims against the executive, alleging breaches of fiduciary duty related to his management of the projects.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined that the corporate entities were not parties to the employment agreement and had not validly ratified it, thereby precluding any contractual liability to the executive for the disputed bonuses or share rewards. Conversely, regarding the entities' claims against the executive, the court found insufficient evidence that his actions constituted a breach of fiduciary duty or directly caused the alleged corporate losses. Consequently, neither the executive's contractual claims against the entities nor the entities' claims against the executive were sustained.
